---
title: "Report title"
subtitle: "*Optional italic subtitle*"
author: "Author"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
    page-layout: full
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
/* ===== Wrap printed Python output (no horizontal scroll for narrative text) =====
   Both <pre> and <pre><code> need targeting; Pandoc nests stdout text inside
   <code>, and Quarto/Bootstrap defaults set `pre { overflow: auto }` which we
   override with !important so wrapping wins. */
pre,
pre code,
.cell-output pre,
.cell-output-stdout pre,
.cell-output-stdout pre code,
.sourceCode pre {
  white-space: pre-wrap !important;
  word-break: break-word !important;
  overflow-x: visible !important;
}

/* ===== Scrollable great_tables container =====
   Each great_tables output is wrapped in <div class="gt-scroll"> via the
   show_table() helper.  The outer .gt-scroll is the SOLE scroll container;
   we neutralize the inner gt div's inline overflow:auto so sticky thead
   works against the outer wrapper instead of the inner one. */
.gt-scroll {
  max-height: 560px !important;
  max-width: 100% !important;
  overflow: auto !important;
  border: 1px solid #ddd;
  margin-bottom: 1em;
  position: relative;
}
.gt-scroll > div {
  overflow: visible !important;
  height: auto !important;
  padding: 0 !important;
}
.gt-scroll .gt_table {
  font-size: 11px !important;
  /* Fill the container when narrower than the viewport, but never squish
     columns below their natural no-wrap width.  When content needs more
     room than the container, min-width:max-content drives horizontal
     scroll on the outer .gt-scroll. */
  width: 100% !important;
  min-width: max-content !important;
  margin: 0 !important;
}
.gt-scroll .gt_table th,
.gt-scroll .gt_table td {
  padding: 3px 8px !important;
  white-space: nowrap !important;
  line-height: 1.25 !important;
}

/* ===== Sticky thead =====
   Apply sticky to <th> cells (more reliable than <tr>).  Title/subtitle
   <th> cells are NOT sticky -- they scroll out as the user scrolls down,
   leaving only the spanner row (when present) and the column-label row
   pinned. */
.gt-scroll thead .gt_column_spanner_outer {
  position: sticky !important;
  top: 0;
  background: white;
  z-index: 12;
  box-shadow: 0 1px 0 #ddd;
}
.gt-scroll thead .gt_col_heading {
  position: sticky !important;
  top: 0;
  background: white;
  z-index: 11;
  box-shadow: 0 1px 0 #ddd;
}
/* When a spanner row exists, the column-label row underneath sticks at
   top: 26px (below the spanner row).  But great_tables emits rowspan=2
   <th> cells in the spanner row for any column without a spanner; those
   cells live in the same DOM row as the spanner cells and must stick at
   top: 0 like their spanner siblings.  The :not([rowspan]) selector
   excludes them from the +26px shift. */
.gt-scroll:has(.gt_column_spanner_outer) thead .gt_col_heading:not([rowspan]) {
  top: 26px;
}
</style>
```

```{python}
#| include: false
from pathlib import Path
import polars as pl
import great_tables as gt
from IPython.display import HTML

DATA = Path("data")  # adjust to your project layout

# Optional: shorten verbose published category/industry names to match the
# source paper's display conventions.  Override the dict per-project.
DISPLAY_NAME_OVERRIDES: dict[int, str] = {
    # 28: "Industrial Mining",
    # 38: "Meas. & Control Equipment",
}


def show_table(gt_obj, max_height: str = "560px") -> HTML:
    """Wrap a great_tables in <div class="gt-scroll"> so long tables get a
    vertical scroll bar with a sticky thead, and wide tables get a
    horizontal scroll bar without disrupting the column headers."""
    return HTML(
        f'<div class="gt-scroll" style="max-height:{max_height}">'
        f'{gt_obj.as_raw_html()}'
        f'</div>'
    )


# Compact-row option set applied to every great_tables table in the report.
COMPACT_OPTS = dict(
    table_font_size="11px",
    data_row_padding="2px",
    column_labels_padding="3px",
    heading_padding="3px",
    row_group_padding="2px",
    table_layout="auto",
)
```

# 1. Overview

Replace this section with the report's headline result in one short paragraph.

# 2. Methodology

Describe data sources, sample window, key methodological choices, and any
discretionary decisions that affect the output.

# 3. Results

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Example: load a parquet, format, and render.
# df = pl.read_parquet(DATA / "results.parquet")
#
# show_table(
#     gt.GT(df)
#     .tab_header(title="Results", subtitle="Brief description of units")
#     .cols_label(col_a="Label A", col_b="Label B")
#     .fmt_number(columns=["pct_col"], decimals=0)
#     .fmt_date(columns="date_col", date_style="iso")
#     .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
#     .tab_options(**COMPACT_OPTS)
# )
```

## Comparison-style table (with spanners)

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Example: side-by-side comparison of two versions of paired metrics.
#
# show_table(
#     gt.GT(df_compare)
#     .tab_header(title="Published vs replicated", subtitle="...")
#     .tab_spanner(label="12m raw",        columns=["x_pub", "x_repl"])
#     .tab_spanner(label="24m raw",        columns=["y_pub", "y_repl"])
#     .tab_spanner(label="24m max DD",     columns=["dd_pub", "dd_repl"])
#     .cols_label(
#         label_col="Series",
#         x_pub="pub",   x_repl="repl",
#         y_pub="pub",   y_repl="repl",
#         dd_pub="pub",  dd_repl="repl",
#     )
#     .fmt_number(columns=[c for c in df_compare.columns if c.endswith(("_pub", "_repl"))], decimals=0)
#     .fmt_date(columns="date_col", date_style="iso")
#     .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
#     .tab_options(**COMPACT_OPTS)
# )
```

# 4. Discussion

Describe what the results mean, where they diverge from prior work, and any
limitations the reader should know about.

# Sources

- [Reference 1](#)
- [Reference 2](#)
